stem: A 33-year-old woman attends 6 weeks after a third consecutive first-trimester miscarriage with her partner. All three losses were at 8 to 10 weeks; the most recent had a sac with no embryo seen and there was no chromosomal sample taken. She is otherwise well, has regular cycles and her BMI is 24. She has had two unprovoked late-term DVTs and is anxious about further losses.

data_table.Examination: Cardiovascular and obstetric examination unremarkable; no skin lesions
data_table.Investigations: TSH 2.1 mIU/L, HbA1c 33 mmol/mol, pelvic ultrasound: normal uterus with no Mullerian anomaly

lead_in: Which investigation should be prioritised at this visit?

options.text: Endometrial biopsy for chronic endometritis with CD138 immunohistochemistry staining
options.rationale: Chronic endometritis is a contested cause; biopsy is not first-line and yields modest evidence in AU practice. (Source: RANZCOG recurrent miscarriage 2023)

options.text: Parental karyotype testing of both partners for balanced translocation screening currently
options.rationale: Parental karyotype yields 2 to 5 percent positivity; product-of-conception karyotype on a future loss is higher-yield and lower-cost as first investigation. (Source: RANZCOG recurrent miscarriage 2023)

options.text: Hysterosalpingography to evaluate the uterine cavity for an intrauterine adhesion or septum
options.rationale: Pelvic ultrasound has already excluded a Mullerian anomaly; HSG adds little when sonohysterography or 3D ultrasound is normal. (Source: RANZCOG recurrent miscarriage 2023)

options.text: Antiphospholipid antibody panel (lupus anticoagulant, anticardiolipin, anti-beta2-glycoprotein-I)
options.rationale: Antiphospholipid syndrome causes 15 percent of recurrent miscarriage; her two unprovoked DVTs further raise pre-test probability and treatment with aspirin plus LMWH improves live-birth rates. (Source: RANZCOG recurrent miscarriage 2023; ASTH 2023)

options.text: Inherited thrombophilia screen (Factor V Leiden, prothrombin G20210A, antithrombin, protein C/S)
options.rationale: Inherited thrombophilias have a weak association with first-trimester recurrent miscarriage and treatment does not improve live birth; routine screening is not recommended. (Source: RANZCOG recurrent miscarriage 2023)

explanation.summary: Recurrent miscarriage (three or more consecutive losses before 20 weeks) affects around 1 percent of couples. AU guidance prioritises antiphospholipid syndrome screening (lupus anticoagulant, anticardiolipin IgG/IgM, anti-beta2-glycoprotein-I IgG/IgM) on two occasions 12 weeks apart, pelvic anatomy assessment, and thyroid and diabetes screening. Inherited thrombophilia screening is not routinely advised. Treatment with aspirin plus prophylactic LMWH improves live-birth rates in confirmed APS by around 40 percent.
key_points: Recurrent miscarriage = three or more consecutive losses before 20 weeks.
First-line screen: APS, pelvic anatomy, TSH, diabetes.
Confirm APS antibodies on two occasions 12 weeks apart at qualifying titres.
Inherited thrombophilia screen NOT recommended for recurrent miscarriage.
APS treatment: aspirin 100 mg from conception + LMWH from positive UPT.
explanation.pearls: Beta2-glycoprotein-I IgG titre above 99th centile predicts the highest pregnancy-loss risk in APS; couples with triple-positive serology have around 30 percent live-birth rate even with aspirin plus LMWH and warrant tertiary referral.

stem: A 35-year-old G3P2 woman at 35+2 weeks presents with two days of bilateral hand swelling and intermittent epigastric pain. She has had three blood pressure readings in clinic ranging from 134 to 142 systolic and 84 to 89 diastolic over the past week. Urine dipstick at this visit is trace protein; she has had no headache, no visual disturbance and the foetus is moving normally. There is no past hypertension.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 138/89, HR 88, T 36.6°C, RR 16; reflexes brisk but no clonus
data_table.Examination: Mild bilateral pitting oedema, epigastric tenderness, fundal height 33 cm (small for dates), FHR 142
data_table.Investigations: Platelets 95 x10^9/L (was 220 at booking), creatinine 88 micromol/L (was 56), AST 78 U/L, ALT 64 U/L, LDH 380 U/L, spot urine PCR 38 mg/mmol

lead_in: Which assessment most accurately characterises this presentation?

options.text: Gestational hypertension only as her blood pressure remains under 140/90 mmHg today
options.rationale: Multi-system involvement (thrombocytopenia, doubled creatinine, transaminitis, proteinuria) plus BP rising establishes pre-eclampsia even without sustained 140/90 readings. (Source: SOMANZ hypertension in pregnancy 2024)

options.text: Atypical pre-eclampsia with HELLP-spectrum features warranting admission and delivery planning
options.rationale: Atypical pre-eclampsia is recognised on multi-system features without classical BP 140/90; doubled creatinine, platelets dropping under 100, transaminitis and epigastric pain meet HELLP-spectrum criteria mandating delivery preparation at 35 weeks. (Source: SOMANZ 2024)

options.text: Acute fatty liver of pregnancy with secondary blood pressure and renal involvement
options.rationale: AFLP typically shows much higher transaminases, hypoglycaemia, raised ammonia and coagulopathy; this picture is HELLP-spectrum not AFLP. (Source: SOMANZ 2024)

options.text: Thrombotic thrombocytopenic purpura with pregnancy-related microangiopathic haemolysis
options.rationale: TTP would show much lower platelets, schistocytes, neurological signs and very high LDH; this constellation is far more consistent with HELLP. (Source: SOMANZ 2024)

options.text: Chronic hypertension with superimposed proteinuria from incidental urinary tract infection
options.rationale: No prior hypertension and a previously normal platelet count and creatinine make superimposed PE on chronic hypertension less likely; an isolated UTI does not explain transaminitis. (Source: SOMANZ 2024)

explanation.summary: SOMANZ 2024 recognises atypical pre-eclampsia where end-organ damage (thrombocytopenia, doubling of creatinine, hepatic transaminitis, neurological symptoms, pulmonary oedema, or foetal growth restriction) develops without sustained BP 140/90. HELLP-spectrum (haemolysis, elevated liver enzymes, low platelets) is a severe variant. Definitive management at 34+ weeks is delivery (vaginal if feasible) with magnesium sulfate for seizure prophylaxis, BP control to under 160/110 and high-dependency monitoring. Steroids for foetal lung maturity remain indicated under 34+6 weeks; corticosteroids do not delay delivery.
key_points: Atypical pre-eclampsia = end-organ damage without classical BP >= 140/90.
HELLP spectrum: any of platelets under 100, transaminases doubled, microangiopathy.
Delivery at 34+ weeks for severe PE / HELLP.
Magnesium sulfate 4 g IV load then 1 g/h for 24 hours post-delivery.
Antihypertensive target under 160/110 acutely with labetalol/hydralazine.
explanation.pearls: Recurrence risk of severe pre-eclampsia in a future pregnancy is around 15 percent; prophylactic aspirin 150 mg from 12 weeks reduces recurrence by 60 percent if started before 16 weeks.

stem: A 28-year-old G2P1 woman who is blood group O Rh-negative attends ED with light fresh vaginal bleeding at 13+2 weeks. Her partner is known to be Rh-positive. She has had no previous sensitising events and her booking antibody screen 6 weeks ago was negative. The bleeding has settled but she remains anxious about pregnancy loss and Rh sensitisation.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 118/72, HR 80, T 36.5°C; abdomen soft non-tender
data_table.Examination: Speculum: small amount of old blood, cervix closed; uterus consistent with dates
data_table.Investigations: Transvaginal ultrasound: viable intrauterine pregnancy, CRL 76 mm, FHR 158; rhesus screen confirmed Rh-negative; antibody screen pending

lead_in: Which step regarding Rh-isoimmunisation prevention is most appropriate?

options.text: No Anti-D required as bleeding was very light and the pregnancy remains viable today
options.rationale: Bleeding at or after 12 weeks of gestation in an Rh-negative woman is a potentially sensitising event regardless of volume; Anti-D is required. (Source: NBA Anti-D guidelines 2021)

options.text: Anti-D 250 IU intramuscularly now without need for Kleihauer testing today
options.rationale: 250 IU is the standard dose only before 12 weeks; after 12 weeks the dose is 625 IU and Kleihauer is required if at 20+ weeks. (Source: NBA Anti-D 2021)

options.text: Anti-D 1500 IU intramuscularly with mandatory Kleihauer-Betke testing today
options.rationale: 1500 IU is the routine antenatal prophylaxis dose at 28 and 34 weeks; for a first-trimester sensitising event 625 IU suffices. (Source: NBA Anti-D 2021)

options.text: Anti-D 625 IU intramuscularly within 72 hours and routine antenatal doses at 28 and 34 weeks
options.rationale: Sensitising event after 12 weeks requires Anti-D 625 IU within 72 hours plus continuation of routine 28 and 34 week prophylaxis; Kleihauer becomes mandatory only after 20 weeks. (Source: NBA Anti-D 2021)

options.text: Withhold Anti-D until paternal genotyping confirms his Rh status and zygosity definitively
options.rationale: AU practice does not delay Anti-D for paternal genotyping; cffDNA foetal Rh typing is available in some centres but does not delay administration. (Source: NBA Anti-D 2021)

explanation.summary: Anti-D prophylaxis prevents Rh-D sensitisation in Rh-negative women carrying an Rh-positive foetus. AU NBA dose schedule: 250 IU IM within 72 hours of a potentially sensitising event before 12 weeks, 625 IU after 12 weeks, plus routine antenatal 625 IU at 28 and 34 weeks (since 2023 a single 1500 IU dose at 28 weeks is an accepted alternative in some centres). Kleihauer-Betke quantifies fetomaternal haemorrhage and is required for sensitising events at 20 weeks or later, after delivery, or after significant trauma to guide whether additional Anti-D is needed beyond the standard dose.
key_points: Anti-D before 12 weeks: 250 IU IM within 72 hours.
Anti-D after 12 weeks: 625 IU IM within 72 hours.
Routine antenatal doses at 28 and 34 weeks (or 1500 IU once at 28 weeks).
Kleihauer-Betke required from 20 weeks and at delivery.
Maternal antibody screen at booking and 28 weeks.
explanation.pearls: If anti-D administration is delayed beyond 72 hours after a sensitising event, give it as soon as possible up to 10 days later - efficacy decreases progressively but partial protection is better than none.

stem: A 30-year-old G2P1 woman has her morphology ultrasound at 22 weeks following IVF conception. She is asymptomatic. The foetus is structurally normal. A repeat targeted transvaginal scan at 32 weeks now confirms a velamentous cord insertion with a single vessel crossing the internal cervical os, consistent with type I vasa praevia. She has no antepartum bleeding and the foetus is growing on the 60th centile.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 116/72, HR 78, fundal height 32 cm, FHR 142
data_table.Examination: Soft non-tender abdomen, no cervical change clinically
data_table.Investigations: Transvaginal ultrasound 32 weeks: foetal vessel crosses internal os; cervix 32 mm long; estimated foetal weight 1850 g (60th centile)

lead_in: Which antenatal plan is most appropriate?

options.text: Plan elective caesarean section at 34 to 36 weeks after antenatal corticosteroids for foetal lungs
options.rationale: Confirmed vasa praevia is delivered by elective LSCS at 34 to 36 weeks with prior antenatal corticosteroids; balance is foetal exsanguination risk against prematurity. (Source: RANZCOG vasa praevia 2023; Queensland Clinical Guideline)

options.text: Plan for vaginal birth with continuous CTG and immediate caesarean if FHR abnormality
options.rationale: Vasa praevia carries up to 60 percent foetal mortality with vaginal birth due to foetal exsanguination on ROM; vaginal trial is unsafe. (Source: RANZCOG vasa praevia 2023)

options.text: Plan elective caesarean at 39 weeks once the pregnancy is at full term gestation
options.rationale: Late preterm delivery (34 to 36 weeks) balances ROM-related foetal death risk against prematurity; waiting to 39 weeks increases the risk of spontaneous ROM and foetal exsanguination. (Source: RANZCOG vasa praevia 2023)

options.text: Admit for inpatient monitoring from 28 weeks with daily CTG until delivery is planned
options.rationale: Routine inpatient admission is not required when there is no bleeding or cervical change; admission is reserved for short cervix or threatened preterm labour. (Source: RANZCOG 2023)

options.text: Repeat ultrasound at 36 weeks to assess for vessel migration before deciding on mode of birth
options.rationale: Vasa praevia type I (velamentous cord) does not migrate like a low-lying placenta; deferring decisions risks spontaneous ROM. (Source: RANZCOG 2023)

explanation.summary: Vasa praevia is the presence of unprotected foetal vessels traversing the membranes within 2 cm of the internal cervical os. It is classified as type I (velamentous cord insertion) or type II (vessels between placental lobes). Antenatal diagnosis at morphology with transvaginal confirmation is essential because intrapartum rupture causes rapid foetal exsanguination. AU guidance recommends elective caesarean at 34 to 36 weeks after a course of corticosteroids. Antenatal admission is reserved for women with short cervix, preterm contractions or any bleeding.
key_points: Vasa praevia = unprotected foetal vessels within 2 cm of internal os.
Mortality with vaginal birth approaches 60 percent.
Risk factors: IVF, velamentous cord, succenturiate lobe, multiple pregnancy.
Plan LSCS at 34 to 36 weeks with antenatal corticosteroids.
Admit if short cervix, preterm labour or any antepartum haemorrhage.
explanation.pearls: If amniotomy is inadvertently performed in undiagnosed vasa praevia, expect bright-red bleeding immediately followed by sinusoidal CTG - this is foetal not maternal blood and demands immediate caesarean delivery.

stem: A 29-year-old G1P0 woman attends her dating scan at 12+1 weeks following spontaneous conception. The sonographer reports two viable fetuses with concordant CRLs of 56 and 58 mm. The lambda (twin peak) sign is clearly visible at the placental junction and the intertwin membrane is thick. The patient is well and has no significant past medical history.

data_table.Examination: Soft abdomen, no peripheral oedema, gravid uterus consistent with twin pregnancy
data_table.Investigations: Ultrasound: two viable fetuses, lambda sign positive, intertwin membrane thickness 2.2 mm, two placental masses described

lead_in: Which conclusion about chorionicity is most accurate?

options.text: Monochorionic monoamniotic twins requiring weekly ultrasound from 16 weeks onwards
options.rationale: MCMA twins share a single placenta with no dividing membrane; the visible lambda sign and thick membrane exclude MCMA. (Source: RANZCOG multiple pregnancy 2024)

options.text: Monochorionic diamniotic twins with a planned scan schedule every two weeks from 16 weeks
options.rationale: MCDA twins show a T-sign and thin membrane; the lambda sign and thick membrane here exclude monochorionic. (Source: RANZCOG multiple pregnancy 2024)

options.text: Conjoined twins requiring tertiary referral for early MRI to delineate organ sharing pattern
options.rationale: Conjoined twins show shared body parts on ultrasound; separate sacs with a thick dividing membrane exclude this. (Source: RANZCOG 2024)

options.text: Dichorionic diamniotic twins requiring growth scans every 4 weeks from 24 weeks onwards
options.rationale: Lambda sign, thick membrane and two placental masses confirm DCDA; AU growth scans are 4-weekly from 24 weeks with delivery offered from 37 weeks. (Source: RANZCOG multiple pregnancy 2024)

options.text: Chorionicity cannot be assigned reliably until 16 to 18 weeks of gestation by ultrasound
options.rationale: Chorionicity is most accurately assigned at 11 to 14 weeks; deferring decreases accuracy. (Source: RANZCOG 2024)

explanation.summary: First-trimester chorionicity determination (11 to 14 weeks) is critical because it dictates scan frequency, complications profile, and timing of delivery. The lambda (twin peak) sign denotes dichorionic (separate placentas meeting); the T-sign denotes monochorionic (single placenta with a thin membrane). Monochorionic twins carry risks of twin-twin transfusion syndrome, twin anaemia-polycythaemia sequence, and selective growth restriction, mandating 2-weekly ultrasound from 16 weeks. DCDA twins need 4-weekly growth scans from 24 weeks and are delivered from 37 weeks.
key_points: Determine chorionicity at 11 to 14 weeks for accuracy.
Lambda sign = DCDA; T-sign = monochorionic.
MCDA: 2-weekly scans from 16 weeks; delivery 36 to 37 weeks.
DCDA: 4-weekly scans from 24 weeks; delivery 37 to 38 weeks.
MCMA: rare, weekly scans from 16 to 18 weeks, delivery by caesarean at 32 to 34 weeks.
explanation.pearls: Unequal CRL discordance of more than 10 percent at 11 to 14 weeks is a strong predictor of TTTS in monochorionic twins and warrants tertiary maternal-foetal medicine referral, regardless of subsequent absolute growth measurements.
